assignment_description:
Botany Literature
⍞ Materials: What a Plant Knows

➜ INTRO
At least one day each week will be spent reading important works of botanical literature. The lesson plans give you an idea of how many pages to read each week to finish the book by the end of the term. If one day is not enough, you might read about half on one day and half on the flex day. If one day is enough, then decide how you would like to use your flex day. Consider the ideas provided on Day 4 of the week.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Read about 14 pages per week; p.3-17.

➜ WRITE
Write a narration in your Botany Notebook.

• PLAN WEEKLY
⬚ Nature walk - Look for the plants and/or features mentioned in your books. Record observations.
⬚ science free read

How Plants Work, Life Cycle of Flowering Plants
⍞ Materials: Elementary Studies in Plant Life

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1. Work through the material, adding drawings to your notebook. You may need to visit local nurseries, botanical gardens, neighbors' yards, and gardens to find specimens to examine. Pick a question at the end to answer fully in your notebook for your narration.

Identification
⍞ Materials: Botany in a Day

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Forward, Region Covered, and p.1-9

➜ NOTE
You may or may not agree with the author's presentation of the evolution of plants. Still, it is important to know current botanical thinking, especially as we seek to understand how plants have been organized. This will help us as we begin to learn plant families.

Flex Day
⍞ Materials: Selected botany literature OR lab/field supplies

➜ INTRO
Use this flex day to finish the week's reading from What a Plant Knows, or continue any lab/field activities. Suppose you have completed the week's activities. In that case, you may use this day to tailor the course to be more literary by reading an additional text, such as Growing a Revolution, or more active by pursuing a special study of interest.

Special Study
⍞ Materials: The Milkweed Lands

➜ INTRO
Notice how the author and illustrator make a study of and learn so much about this one plant. Whether you have milkweed near you and can notice their observations, or you make a similar study of a particular plant in your locale, enjoy their method as you develop your own.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Half of Ch.1 (to the end of "Dangerous Colors" p.8)

How Plants Work, Soil Science
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ INTRO
Scientists now know that soil science is fundamentally important to plant growth and even human health. This is because of the vast number of organisms that live in the soil food web. Look at a basic diagram of this web at the link and copy it into your Botany Notebook. Then, watch the videos to consider how it works and why it's important.

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Soil Food Web

➜ VIEW, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
∞ Video Link: The Living Soil Beneath Our Feet (2:49)
∞ Video Link: How Regenerative Agriculture Brings Life Back (15:22)

How Plants Work, Leaf Anatomy
⍞ Materials: Elementary Studies in Plant Life, microscope, and prepared slides

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.6

• Using the prepared slide set, examine Slide 12, the Hydrilla verticillata whole mount. Notice the form of the leaf, the features along the margin, the tiny cells that repeat over and over, and anything else that stands out. You will learn more about cells in General Biology. Also, examine Slide 1, the pine leaf cross-section. A cross-section is a slice perpendicular to the length. Find the outer layer, called the epidermis, and the tiny pores through the epidermis that allow the leaf to breathe, called the stomata. Record what you see in your notebook.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Botany Lessons — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Day 1:
• What a Plant Knows: Pick one aspect of things that a "plant knows" and write about it.

· Day 2:
• Elementary Studies: Explain what you know about photosynthesis. Use sketches or diagrams.
• Elementary Studies: What are the different parts of a flower, and what are they used for? Use sketches or diagrams.

· Day 3:
• Botany in a Day: Outline with words and sketches the 8 common plant families we studied and how to recognize them.

· Day 4:
• The Milkweed Lands: Write a summary OR draw/diagram a 4-panel illustration of the ecological role that milkweed plays over the seasons.

· Day 5:
• Practical work or links: Describe with words and sketches your observations of 2 local plants OR discuss the importance of soil quality.

Botany Literature
⍞ Materials: The Hidden Life of Trees: The Illustrated Edition

➜ INTRO
To ensure that the beautiful photography in this book is inspiring rather than distracting, take a few moments to look at the pictures on the pages you will read before beginning to read. The lesson plans give you an idea of how many pages to read each week to finish the book by the end of the term. If one day is not enough, then you might read about half on one day and half on the flex day. If one day is enough, then decide how you would like to use your flex day. Consider the ideas provided on Day 4 of the week.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Read about 14 pages per week; p.1-12.

➜ WRITE
Write a narration in your Botany Notebook.

• PLAN WEEKLY
⬚ Nature walk - look for the plants and/or features mentioned in your botany books, record observations; collect twigs and seeds
⬚ science free read

How Plants Work, Herbs in Winter
⍞ Materials: Elementary Studies in Plant Life

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.9

Identification
⍞ Materials: Botany in a Day, a few different types of flowers

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.22-25. Begin to familiarize yourself with the keys on p.26-35 (you will have another week to do this). You may find it helpful to have a few different types of flowers to look at while examining the keys. The goal is to understand how they work and why we use them, rather than to memorize information.

➜ VIEW
The last 10 minutes of this video are on identifying new plants.
∞ Video Link: Plant ID (38:50-46:03)

★ STUDENT TIP
Finding flowers this time of year may require purchasing or asking for old ones at local florists. Once you get used to the way keys work, you will be ready to start using keys to ID trees that are around in winter. If you have trouble identifying a tree, move on to another tree, get help from local experts, use your Tree in Winter book, search online for local winter tree ID help, or find a local naturalist to ask.

∞ Link: Winter Twig Key Example (a web-based key)

Identification
⍞ Materials: Botany in a Day, The Sibley Guide to Trees

➜ READ
Read about the Fagaceae family on p.100 of Botany in a Day. Members of the 3 Beech family genera can be compared in The Sibley Guide on p.172-226. Which of these is most widespread in North America? What family characteristics do these different genera share? What distinguishes them? Find 2-3 species in the family found in your area to read more about.

➜ PRACTICE
Observe your collection of seeds. Can you categorize them according to the different types described in your guide? Can you identify the plant from which each one came? What type of habitat does the parent plant live in? Or in what habitat did you find the seed? Based on the features of the seed, what is its mechanism of spreading? How do you suppose this benefits the plant? You will have more time for this observation next week.

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Botany Lessons — TERM 2 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Day 1:
• The Hidden Life of Trees: Pick one aspect of The Hidden Life of Trees and write about it.

· Day 2:
• Elementary Studies and/or practical work: Use words or pictures to contrast the form and function of at least two types of seeds OR describe the process of keying out a flower, twig, or tree.

· Day 3:
• Practical work: Use words and sketches to describe your observations of 2 local trees.

· Day 4:
• Current events: Discuss one current event in botany that you read about this term.

· Day 5:
• Practical work: Present your citizen science project to your teacher.

assignment_description:
Botany Literature
⍞ Materials: Oaxaca Journal

➜ INTRO
The lesson plans give you an idea of how many pages to read each week to finish the book by the end of the term. If one day is not enough, then you might read about half on one day and half on the flex day. If one day is enough, then decide how you would like to use your flex day. Consider the ideas provided on Day 4 of the week.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Read about 14.5 pages per week, to p.15.

➜ DRAW
Make a large sketch of the region where the book takes place. A nice map is on the first page. Then, as you read, fill in interesting things that he describes. At the end, you will have a map that represents some of the mental images that you have of the area after reading his descriptive writing.

• PLAN WEEKLY
⬚ Nature walk - record observations
⬚ science free read

How Plants Work, Germination Part 1
⍞ Materials: Elementary Studies in Plant Life

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.11

➜ REFLECT
If you have already taken Biology, recall what you learned about why oxygen is necessary for cellular respiration. Reflect on why you think the author did not explain all the details in the process.

➜ OBSERVE
Spend the time outside looking at seeds, trees, and plants, recording your observations. You should be able to see so much more now than when you started in the fall!

Flex Day
⍞ Materials: Selected botany literature OR lab/field supplies

➜ NOTE
Use this flex day to finish the week's reading from Oaxaca Journal or continue any lab/field activities. Suppose these activities are finished for the week. In that case, you may use this day to tailor the course to be more literary by reading an additional text, such as Growing a Revolution, or more active by pursuing a special study of interest.

Current Events
⍞ Materials: Links

➜ READ/WRITE
Continue exploring the links from Week 1.

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

• PLAN WEEKLY
⬚ Nature walk - record observations
⬚ science free read
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Botany Lessons — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Day 1:
• Oaxaca Journal: Describe some of the ferns the author saw in Oaxaca and some of his observations.

· Day 2:
• Elementary Studies: Describe the process of germination. How are a corn seed and a bean seed different?

· Day 3:
• Botany in a Day and practical work: What new plants have you seen locally since spring has arrived? How many of the 8 first families that we learned have you found?

· Day 4:
• Botany in a Day and practical work: Describe how you prepared soil samples for microscopic examination and explain any observations you made between different locations or times of year.

· Day 5:
• Practical work: Show your teacher your Botany Notebook. Select a favorite page to tell about and scan.
